Title: Autocatalytic effect boosts the production of medium-chain hydrocarbons by fatty acid photodecarboxylase

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Arginine residue in CvFAP active site
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Critical residue for positioning fatty acid substrate near FAD, thereby enabling efficient hydrocarbon production by CvFAP
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Blue light illumination; fatty acid substrate chain length; added n-alkane cocatalysts
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. R451 maintains close interaction with the carboxyl group of C8 fatty acid during simulations. 2. This interaction supports substrate positioning in the CvFAP active site. 3. Proper positioning is important for productive photodecarboxylation to hydrocarbons.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for protein engineering of substrate positioning and activity tuning
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a catalytic-site residue rather than a separately manipulated gene; included because the paper explicitly identifies it as crucial for substrate positioning.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: E. coli; in vitro purified enzyme system
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key catalytic step for hydrocarbon formation; paper shows substrate-dependent control and enhanced efficiency via autocatalysis/cocatalysis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: n-octanoic acid; n-hexadecanoic acid; C7-C18 fatty acids
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: n-heptane; n-pentadecane; corresponding n-1 alkanes plus CO2
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Light-driven decarboxylation of fatty acids to the corresponding one-carbon-shorter n-alkanes with release of CO2 via an FAD-dependent photochemical mechanism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes highly efficient production of medium-chain hydrocarbons, especially n-heptane from n-octanoic acid, exceeding activity on n-hexadecanoic acid under optimized conditions
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously expressed in E. coli after IPTG induction
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Blue light illumination; pH 6.0 for C8 and pH 8.5 for C16 optimum; n-alkane cocatalyst presence; substrate chain length
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purified enzyme assays; heterologous expression in E. coli; time-resolved fluorescence; transient absorption spectroscopy; molecular dynamics simulations; GC-MS product quantification; 13CO2 analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CvFAP converts n-octanoic acid in vitro about four times faster than n-hexadecanoic acid under optimized conditions. 2. In vivo, CvFAP-expressing E. coli produced almost 13-fold more 13CO2 from C8 than C16 fatty acid, indicating much higher C8 decarboxylation. 3. The n-heptane product acts autocatalytically/cocatalytically by filling the remaining binding pocket and improving medium-chain substrate positioning. 4. Added C5-C12 alkanes can boost quantum yields of C7-C12 fatty acid decarboxylation to about 70%.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for metabolic engineering of bio-based gasoline-range hydrocarbon production; optimize substrate choice, pH, and alkane cocatalyst context; engineer active-site pocket for medium-chain substrates
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Activity strongly depends on substrate chain length, pH, and cocatalyst presence; possible volatility/handling issues of short alkane products
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study used FAPv2, a version truncated by the first 75 amino acids at the N terminus. The main focus is enzymatic and biophysical characterization rather than native physiological function.
